```{r stan-fit, results = "hide"}
  list(alpha = 5.9, beta = c(0.0, 0.0), shape = 1.2,
       t_cens = stan_data$t_cens_lower * 2.0)
}

fit_augmentation <- stan(
  model_code = stan_code, data = stan_data,
  chains = 4, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000, seed = 5440, refresh = 0, init = init_fn
)
```

#### Fit and Diagnostics

```{r stan-diag}
cat("\n===== DIAGNOSTICS: DATA AUGMENTATION =====\n")

params_of_interest <- c("alpha", "beta[1]", "beta[2]", "shape", "med_male", "med_female")
print(fit_augmentation, pars = params_of_interest, probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))

sampler_params <- get_sampler_params(fit_augmentation, inc_warmup = FALSE)
divergences    <- sum(sapply(sampler_params, function(x) sum(x[, "divergent__"])))
cat("\nTotal divergences:", divergences, "\n")

rhats_aug <- summary(fit_augmentation)$summary[params_of_interest, "Rhat"]
cat("Max Rhat (structural params):", round(max(rhats_aug, na.rm = TRUE), 4), "\n")
```

Zero divergent transitions and a maximum R-hat near 1.00 confirm convergence. The augmentation model adds 63 latent event-time parameters — one per censored patient — to the four structural parameters of the marginalization model. This is the computational cost referenced in Section 2.4.3.

#### Trace Plots

```{r stan-trace, fig.width = 10, fig.height = 6}
draws_array <- as.array(fit_augmentation,
                        pars = c("alpha", "beta[1]", "beta[2]", "shape"))

p_trace_aug <- mcmc_trace(draws_array) +
  ggtitle("Trace Plots — Weibull Data Augmentation",
          subtitle = "4 chains, 1000 post-warmup draws each") +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave("augmentation_traceplots.png", p_trace_aug, width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 150)
p_trace_aug
```

#### Posterior of Imputed Event Times

A unique benefit of data augmentation is that we obtain a posterior distribution for each censored patient's event time, ordered here by the censoring threshold.

```{r stan-latent, fig.width = 10, fig.height = 5}
draws_df  <- as.data.frame(fit_augmentation)
cens_cols <- grep("^t_cens\\[", names(draws_df), value = TRUE)

# Posterior median + 95% CrI for each latent event time
latent_summary <- data.frame(
  patient_id  = seq_along(idx_cens),
  cens_lower  = stan_data$t_cens_lower,
  post_median = sapply(cens_cols, function(col) median(draws_df[[col]])),
  post_lower  = sapply(cens_cols, function(col) quantile(draws_df[[col]], 0.025)),
  post_upper  = sapply(cens_cols, function(col) quantile(draws_df[[col]], 0.975))
) %>% arrange(cens_lower)

ggplot(latent_summary, aes(x = reorder(patient_id, cens_lower))) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = post_lower, ymax = post_upper),
                color = col_obs, alpha = 0.5, width = 0) +
  geom_point(aes(y = post_median), color = col_obs, size = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = cens_lower),  color = col_cens, size = 1, shape = 4) +
  labs(title    = "Posterior Distributions of Latent Event Times",
       subtitle = "Blue = posterior median + 95% CrI | Red cross = censoring threshold",
       x = "Censored patients (ordered by censoring time)", y = "Survival time (days)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), axis.ticks.x = element_blank())
```

Every posterior interval lies strictly above its censoring threshold, as required by the model. Patients with later censoring times have posterior medians shifted further to the right, reflecting the fact that they have already survived longer. This plot is unique to data augmentation: marginalization never produces posterior draws of the censored patients' event times.

#### Method Comparison: Augmentation vs. Marginalization

If the two parameterizations are mathematically equivalent, their posteriors for the structural parameters should agree.

```{r method-compare, fig.width = 8, fig.height = 5}
draws_marg    <- as_draws_df(fit_weibull)
beta_sex_marg <- draws_marg$b_sex2
beta_sex_aug  <- draws_df[["beta[2]"]]

compare_df <- data.frame(
  beta_sex = c(beta_sex_marg, beta_sex_aug),
  Method   = c(rep("Marginalization (brms)",   length(beta_sex_marg)),
               rep("Data Augmentation (Stan)", length(beta_sex_aug)))
)

ggplot(compare_df, aes(x = beta_sex, fill = Method)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Marginalization (brms)"   = col_marg,
                               "Data Augmentation (Stan)" = col_aug)) +
  labs(title    = "Posterior of beta_sex: Augmentation vs. Marginalization",
       subtitle = "Both methods should produce identical posteriors",
       x = "beta_sex (female vs. male, log-time scale)", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

cat("\n===== METHOD COMPARISON: beta_sex =====\n")
cat("Marginalization - Mean:", round(mean(beta_sex_marg), 3),
    " 95% CrI:", round(quantile(beta_sex_marg, 0.025), 3),
    "-", round(quantile(beta_sex_marg, 0.975), 3), "\n")
cat("Augmentation   - Mean:", round(mean(beta_sex_aug), 3),
    " 95% CrI:", round(quantile(beta_sex_aug, 0.025), 3),
    "-", round(quantile(beta_sex_aug, 0.975), 3), "\n")

cat("\nData augmentation introduces", length(idx_cens), "latent parameters.\n")
cat("Marginalization uses 0 latent parameters.\n")
cat("Both target the same posterior for alpha, beta, shape.\n")
```

Marginalization and data augmentation produce essentially identical posterior densities for $\beta_{\text{sex}}$, with posterior means agreeing to within 0.005. This is exactly what theory predicts: the two parameterizations are mathematically equivalent. The choice between them is therefore driven by engineering, not statistics. Marginalization is simpler and faster when a closed-form CDF is available; data augmentation is more flexible for custom likelihoods and additionally yields posterior draws of the imputed event times.

### 5 Sensitivity Analysis: What If We Had Ignored Censoring?

The most consequential modeling decision in any survival analysis is whether to handle censoring at all. To make this concrete, we now fit a naive Weibull regression that treats every recorded time as an exact event time, then compare its posterior to the censoring-aware approaches.

```{r naive-fit, results = "hide"}
# Naive: same priors and family but no cens() handling
fit_fixed <- brm(
  formula = time ~ age + sex,
  family  = weibull(),
  data    = lung,
  prior   = c(
    prior(normal(5.9, 1), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 0.5), class = "b"),
    prior(gamma(2, 2),    class = "shape")
  ),
  chains = 4, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000, seed = 5440, refresh = 0
)
```

```{r naive-compare, fig.width = 8, fig.height = 5}
draws_marg_plot <- as_draws_df(fit_weibull) %>%
  transmute(method = "Censoring (Marginalization)", age = b_age, sex = b_sex2)

draws_aug_plot <- as.data.frame(fit_augmentation) %>%
  transmute(method = "Censoring (Data Augmentation)", age = `beta[1]`, sex = `beta[2]`)

draws_fixed_plot <- as_draws_df(fit_fixed) %>%
  transmute(method = "Ignore Censoring (Naive)", age = b_age, sex = b_sex2)

# Pool the three sets of draws and summarise to mean + 95% CrI
coef_plot_data <- bind_rows(draws_marg_plot, draws_aug_plot, draws_fixed_plot) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(age, sex), names_to = "term", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(method, term) %>%
  summarise(mean  = mean(value),
            lower = quantile(value, 0.025),
            upper = quantile(value, 0.975),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(term = recode(term, age = "Age", sex = "Sex (Female vs. Male)"))

ggplot(coef_plot_data,
       aes(x = term, y = mean, ymin = lower, ymax = upper, color = method)) +
  geom_pointrange(position = position_dodge(width = 0.45), linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey40") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Censoring (Marginalization)"   = col_marg,
                                "Censoring (Data Augmentation)" = col_aug,
                                "Ignore Censoring (Naive)"      = col_naive)) +
  labs(title = "Posterior Regression Coefficients: Censoring vs. Ignoring Censoring",
       x = NULL, y = "Posterior estimate with 95% CrI", color = NULL) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)
```

The naive model that treats censored observations as fully observed event times severely underestimates the sex effect: its posterior mean shrinks by roughly 63%, and its 95% credible interval crosses zero, leading to the qualitatively misleading conclusion that there is no clear sex effect. This happens because female patients are over-represented among the censored group — they survive longer and are more likely to be alive at study end — so pretending their censoring times are death times artificially shrinks the female-male difference toward zero. The lesson generalizes: failing to model censoring is not a conservative choice but a wrong one, and it can flip the direction of the substantive conclusion.

##  Discussion

### What Did We Learn?

Females have approximately 44% longer median survival than males of the same age (Weibull AFT: median 368 vs. 256 days; $\beta_{\text{sex}}$ posterior mean 0.37, 95% CrI 0.14 to 0.62, $P(\beta_{\text{sex}} > 0) \approx 1$). The age effect is small, with a 95% credible interval that nearly contains zero ($\beta_{\text{age}}$ approximately $-0.013$, 95% CrI $-0.029$ to $0.001$); this may reflect limited power rather than a true null effect.

Marginalization and data augmentation produce essentially identical posteriors for the structural parameters ($\beta_{\text{sex}}$: 0.367 vs. 0.363), as theory predicts. Marginalization is simpler and faster, while data augmentation additionally yields imputed event times for censored patients.

### Limitations

Several caveats deserve emphasis. The parametric family is an untestable assumption — both the Weibull and the log-normal fit the observed data but extrapolate differently. The covariate set is also limited: it likely omits performance status (`ph.ecog`), a strong prognostic indicator whose absence may inflate the apparent sex effect. The non-informative censoring assumption cannot be tested directly with these data. Finally, the analysis assumes that sex and age act additively on log survival time with a linear age effect, which biology does not require.

### When Should We Use This Model?

The Weibull AFT with marginalization is a sensible default when the outcome is a positive continuous time-to-event, some observations are censored, a monotone hazard is plausible, and covariate effects are best expressed as multiplicative changes in survival time. The log-normal AFT is preferable when a non-monotone hazard is expected. Cox proportional hazards avoids specifying the baseline hazard parametrically. Stan data augmentation becomes preferable when the censored likelihood lacks a closed-form CDF or when posterior draws of imputed event times are needed downstream.

# 3. Summary and Takeaways

In this tutorial we have studied missing data and censored data as two versions of the same underlying problem: in both cases the missingness mechanism cannot be ignored, and it directly affects the validity of inference. Throughout, we have argued that a Bayesian approach addresses this issue by incorporating the missingness mechanism into the model itself. The strategy can be summarized in three steps.

First, we augment the model. This involves expanding the parameter space to treat the gaps in our data as latent variables — unknowns that are estimated alongside our model parameters.

Second, we constrain these gaps using the available information. For missing data, we leverage the joint distribution and correlations between observed and unobserved variables to inform the missing values. For censored data, we apply known physical or temporal boundaries; for example, if a study ends before an event occurs, we constrain the latent variable by the fact that it must fall in the future ($Y_i > c_i$).

Finally, we propagate that uncertainty. By marginalizing over the posterior distribution of these augmented values, the model ensures that the final estimates for our parameters account for the fact that some of the data are also estimated.

It is worth noting that these solutions rely on identifying assumptions about the nature of the missingness that cannot be verified from the observed data alone. For missing data we typically assume Missing at Random, where the probability of missingness depends only on observed data. For censored data we assume non-informative censoring, where the reason for censoring is independent of the event time itself.

Because standard posterior predictive checks are restricted to the data we can actually see, we are inherently unable to validate these assumptions about the unobserved mechanism. To address this, we rely on sensitivity analysis — perturbing our assumptions and observing whether our conclusions remain robust or shift. This approach is a transparent way to demonstrate the stability of our results.

## Decision Guide

```{r decision-table, echo=FALSE}
decision_df <- data.frame(
  Situation = c(
    "Missing data, many mixed-type predictors",
    "Missing outcome, or joint posterior over $(\\theta, y^{\\text{mis}})$ needed",
    "MNAR concern",
    "Censored data, closed-form CDF available",
    "Censored data, no closed-form CDF, or imputed event times needed",
    "Informative censoring"
  ),
  `Recommended approach` = c(
    "`mice` + `brm_multiple()`",
    "Stan data augmentation",
    "Selection or pattern-mixture model with sensitivity analysis",
    "Marginalization (`brms cens()`, `lccdf`)",
    "Stan data augmentation",
    "Joint model of event and censoring processes"
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)

style_table(
  kable(decision_df, escape = FALSE, align = "ll",
        col.names = c("Situation", "Recommended approach"))
) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "22em") %>%
  column_spec(2, width = "22em")
```

## Session Information

```{r session-info}
sessionInfo()
```
